events.title: Fall of Edessa to Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur
events.region: County of Edessa, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Şanlıurfa, Turkey)
events.description: On 28 December 1144, Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur, atabeg of Mosul and Aleppo, captured the Crusader County of Edessa after a siege. Edessa was the northernmost and oldest of the Crusader States, founded during the First Crusade in 1098. Its fall represented the first major Crusader loss since the establishment of the Crusader States and shocked Western Christendom. The county's ruler, Joscelin II of Courtenay, was away from the city when it fell and was unable to mount an effective defense. The loss of Edessa demonstrated the vulnerability of the Crusader presence in the Levant and triggered the papal response that led to the Second Crusade. Zengi's capture of Edessa was a turning point in the balance of power between Crusaders and Muslims in the Levant.
key_figures: Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur (atabeg of Mosul and Aleppo)
Joscelin II of Courtenay (count of Edessa)

sources.name: William of Tyre, Historia Belli Sacri (History of the Holy War)
Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (Al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
Michael the Syrian, Chronicle (Syriac Christian perspective)

events.certainty_note: The fall of Edessa on 28 December 1144 is well-attested in both Latin and Islamic sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: 1145-001 (Papal proclamation of Second Crusade in response)

events.title: Joscelin II of Courtenay loses Edessa; becomes a fugitive
events.description: Following the fall of Edessa to Zengi, Joscelin II of Courtenay, the last count of Edessa, became a fugitive. He had been away from the city when it fell and was unable to recapture it. Joscelin spent the remainder of his life attempting to recover his lost county, but without success. He eventually retired to the Crusader stronghold of Turbessel (Tell Bashir) in northern Syria, where he lived in reduced circumstances. Joscelin's loss of Edessa marked the end of the first Crusader state and demonstrated the precariousness of Crusader rule in the Levant. His failure to recover Edessa also highlighted the limitations of individual Crusader lords in the face of organized Muslim opposition.
key_figures: Joscelin II of Courtenay (count of Edessa)
Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur (conqueror of Edessa)

sources.name: William of Tyre, Historia Belli Sacri
Matthew of Edessa, Armenian Chronicle

events.certainty_note: Joscelin II's loss of Edessa and subsequent fugitive status are well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

The project, begun in 1137, was progressing toward completion of the western façade and narthex. Suger's innovations—pointed arches, ribbed vaults, flying buttresses, and extensive use of stained glass—were creating a revolutionary architectural style that would become known as Gothic. The basilica served as the royal necropolis of French kings and was a center of intellectual and artistic patronage.

events.title: Stephen III reigns as King of Hungary
events.description: Stephen III (also known as Stephen IV) reigned as King of Hungary from 1141 to 1162. In 1144, he was in the middle of his reign, which was marked by consolidation of royal authority, conflicts with the nobility, and expansion of Hungarian influence in the Balkans. Stephen III was a strong ruler who resisted papal interference and maintained Hungary's independence from both the Holy Roman Empire and the Byzantine Empire. His reign was a period of relative stability and prosperity for Hungary.

sources.name: Anonymus, Gesta Hungarorum (Hungarian chronicle, earlier period but relevant context)
Simon of Kéza, Gesta Hungarorum (Hungarian chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Stephen III's reign (1141–1162) is well-attested in Hungarian sources. The year 1144 falls securely within his reign.

events.title: Wanyan Liang becomes Emperor of the Jin dynasty
events.description: In 1144, Wanyan Liang (also known as Jin Xizong) became emperor of the Jin dynasty (Jurchen-ruled northern China). He was a controversial figure who attempted to sinicize the Jin court and culture, adopting Han Chinese customs and administrative practices. Wanyan Liang's reign was marked by military campaigns against the Song dynasty to the south and internal conflicts within the Jin court. His reign lasted until 1161, when he was assassinated during a military campaign. Wanyan Liang's attempts at sinicization reflected the complex cultural dynamics of the Jin dynasty, which ruled over a mixed population of Jurchen, Khitan, and Han Chinese.
key_figures: Wanyan Yong (predecessor, Emperor Taizong of Jin)

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty, compiled in the Yuan dynasty)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty, compiled in the Yuan dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Wanyan Liang's accession to the throne in 1144 is recorded in the Jin Shi. The date is secure in Chinese dynastic records.

disconfirming_evidence: The fall of Edessa is sometimes dated to 28 December 1144, but some sources give the date as 1143 or early 1145. Modern scholarship generally accepts 28 December 1144 as the correct date. The significance of Edessa's fall is sometimes overstated in Western sources as the 'first' Crusader loss, when in fact there had been earlier losses of individual fortresses and territories. However, Edessa was the first major Crusader state to fall entirely, making it symbolically and strategically significant. Stephen III of Hungary's reign dates are sometimes given as 1141–1162, but some sources vary by a year or two. Chinese dynastic records (Jin Shi, Song Shi) were compiled centuries after the events they describe, making dating uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1144 is heavily weighted toward Western European and Islamic sources. The fall of Edessa is well-documented in both Latin chronicles (William of Tyre) and Islamic sources (Ibn al-Athir), though with some differences in emphasis and detail. Syriac Christian sources (Michael the Syrian, Matthew of Edessa) provide additional perspectives on the fall of Edessa. Northern European sources are sparse and rely on later compilations. East Asian sources (Jin Shi, Song Shi) are available but were compiled centuries after the events they describe. Sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and much of the Indian Ocean world are essentially absent from contemporary documentation. The sources reflect the biases of their authors: Latin chronicles emphasize Christian perspectives and often demonize Muslim actors, while Islamic sources may underrepresent Crusader military capabilities. Syriac Christian sources provide a perspective distinct from both Latin and Islamic sources, reflecting the complex religious landscape of the Levant.

events.title: Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur besieges Edessa
events.region: County of Edessa, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Şanlıurfa, Turkey)
events.description: In 1143, Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur, atabeg of Mosul and Aleppo, began a siege of the Crusader County of Edessa. The siege would continue into 1144 and culminate in the fall of the city on 28 December 1144. Edessa was the northernmost and oldest of the Crusader States, founded during the First Crusade in 1098. The siege demonstrated Zengi's military capabilities and his determination to expand Zengid control in northern Syria and Mesopotamia. The eventual fall of Edessa would shock Western Christendom and trigger the Second Crusade.
key_figures: Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur (atabeg of Mosul and Aleppo)
Joscelin II of Courtenay (count of Edessa)

events.title: Pope Innocent II dies; Pope Celestine II elected
events.description: Pope Innocent II died on 24 September 1143, and Pope Celestine II was elected as his successor. Celestine II's papacy was brief (he died in March 1144) and marked by attempts to maintain papal authority in the face of challenges from the Roman commune and the Holy Roman Empire. Innocent II had been a strong pope who had consolidated papal authority and supported the Cistercian order. Celestine II continued these policies but had little time to make a significant impact before his death. The succession of popes in the 1140s reflected the ongoing struggles for power within the Church and between the papacy and secular rulers.
key_figures: Pope Lucius II (successor to Celestine II, elected 1144)

events.title: Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur consolidates control of northern Syria and Mesopotamia
events.region: Aleppo, Mosul, and surrounding territories (Zengid domains)
events.description: In 1142, Zengi ibn Aq Sunqur, atabeg of Mosul and Aleppo, continued to consolidate his control over northern Syria and Mesopotamia. Zengi had founded the Zengid dynasty in the early 12th century and had become the dominant Muslim power in the region. His control extended from Mosul in the east to Aleppo in the west, and he was preparing for military campaigns against the Crusader states. Zengi's consolidation of power represented a significant shift in the balance of power in the Levant, as he unified previously fragmented Muslim territories under his authority. His eventual capture of Edessa in 1144 would demonstrate the military capabilities he had developed during this period of consolidation.

events.title: Pope Innocent II reigns as Pope
events.description: Pope Innocent II reigned as pope from 1130 to 1143. In 1142, he was in the final year of his papacy, which had been marked by consolidation of papal authority, support for the Cistercian order, and conflicts with the Holy Roman Empire over investiture and temporal power. Innocent II had been a strong pope who had defeated the antipope Anacletus II and established papal supremacy within the Church. His reign represented a period of papal strength and influence in Western Christendom. He would die on 24 September 1143.

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (papal chronicle)

events.title: Abelard of Bath dies; end of an era of scholastic philosophy
events.description: Peter Abelard (also known as Abelard of Bath), one of the most influential philosophers and theologians of the 12th century, died in 1142. Abelard had been a controversial figure whose application of logic to theology had challenged traditional Church authority. His method of dialectical reasoning—presenting opposing views and attempting to reconcile them—became foundational to scholastic philosophy. Abelard's death marked the end of an era of early scholasticism, though his intellectual legacy would continue to influence medieval philosophy. His life had been marked by personal tragedy (his castration by the relatives of Héloïse) and intellectual controversy (his condemnation by the Church), but his contributions to medieval thought were profound and lasting.

sources.name: Abelard, Historia Calamitatum (autobiographical account)
Letters of Abelard and Héloïse (correspondence)
John of Salisbury, Metalogicon (account of Abelard's influence)

events.certainty_note: Abelard's death in 1142 is well-attested in contemporary sources, including his own writings and those of his contemporaries.

disconfirming_evidence: Zengi's consolidation of power occurred gradually over several decades, and the year 1142 is somewhat arbitrary as a marker of this process. The exact chronology of his consolidation is not always clear in sources. Peter Abelard's death date is sometimes given as 1142, but some sources place it in 1143 or 1144. Modern scholarship generally accepts 1142 as the correct date, but there is some uncertainty. Chinese dynastic records (Jin Shi, Song Shi) were compiled centuries after the events they describe, making dating uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1142 is heavily weighted toward Western European and Islamic sources. Zengi's consolidation of power is attested in Islamic sources (Ibn al-Athir) and epigraphic evidence (inscriptions and coins). Papal succession is well-documented in papal records and chronicles. Abelard's death is well-attested in contemporary sources, including his own writings and those of his contemporaries. Northern European sources are sparse and rely on later compilations. East Asian sources (Jin Shi, Song Shi) are available but were compiled centuries after the events they describe. Sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and much of the Indian Ocean world are essentially absent from contemporary documentation. The sources reflect the biases of their authors: Latin chronicles emphasize Christian perspectives, while Islamic sources may underrepresent Crusader military capabilities. Abelard's writings provide a unique perspective on 12th-century intellectual life and the challenges to Church authority.

In East Asia, the Jin dynasty controlled northern China while the Southern Song dynasty ruled the south; in Japan, the Heian period was transitioning toward the Kamakura shogunate. Islamic scholarship and mathematics were at a high point, particularly in Al-Andalus and the Levant.

events.title: Battle of the Horns of Hattin (Saladin's preparation phase)
events.description: While the famous Battle of Hattin occurred in 1187, the year 1141 marks a critical phase in the consolidation of Zengid power under Nur ad-Din, who was systematically unifying Muslim forces in Syria and Mesopotamia. This period saw the strengthening of the military infrastructure and alliances that would eventually enable Saladin's later victories. Nur ad-Din's campaigns in 1141 reinforced Zengid control over key Syrian cities and weakened Crusader territorial coherence.
key_figures: Imad ad-Din Zengi (father, deceased 1146 but legacy dominant)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
William of Tyre, Historia

events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's campaigns in this period are well-attested in Islamic and Crusader chronicles, though specific battles and dates vary slightly between sources. The broader strategic consolidation is confirmed.

events.title: Anarchy in England: Stephen vs. Matilda civil war intensifies
events.description: The civil war between King Stephen and the Empress Matilda (also called the Anarchy) reached a critical phase in 1141. Stephen was captured at the Battle of Lincoln in February 1141, a major military defeat that temporarily shifted the balance of power toward Matilda's faction. However, Matilda's subsequent unpopularity in London and her failure to consolidate support prevented her from establishing stable rule, prolonging the conflict until 1153.
key_figures: Robert of Gloucester (Matilda's half-brother, military leader)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (E version)
John of Worcester, Chronicle
Gesta Stephani

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Lincoln and Stephen's capture are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary English chronicles. The date (February 1141) is secure.

events.title: Matilda briefly recognized as Lady of the English
events.description: Following Stephen's capture at Lincoln, Matilda was recognized by the church and nobility as 'Lady of the English' (Domina Anglorum) in 1141, though she was never formally crowned. Her position was undermined by her arrogance and the Londoners' rejection of her authority. By autumn 1141, Stephen's supporters had regrouped, and Matilda's brief ascendancy collapsed, leading to her eventual exile to Normandy.
key_figures: Bishop Henry of Winchester (her brother, initially supporting her)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
Gesta Stephani

events.certainty_note: Matilda's recognition and subsequent loss of support are documented in contemporary English sources.

events.title: Jin dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Jin dynasty, established by the Jurchen people in 1115, continued to consolidate its rule over northern China in 1141. The Treaty of Shaoxing (1141) between the Jin and Southern Song dynasties formally recognized the division of China, with the Jin controlling the north and the Song the south. This treaty, negotiated by the Jin chancellor Wanyan Liang and Southern Song official Qin Hui, established a relatively stable border and tribute relationship that would persist for decades.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Historian) and later compilations
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Shaoxing is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The date 1141 is secure.

events.title: Treaty of Shaoxing: Jin-Song border stabilization
events.description: The Treaty of Shaoxing (also called the Treaty of Longxing) formally ended the Jin-Song conflict and established a stable border along the Huai River. The Song agreed to pay annual tribute to the Jin and recognized Jin sovereignty over northern China. This treaty was a major diplomatic achievement that allowed both dynasties to consolidate their territories and focus on internal development. The treaty held until the Jin dynasty's collapse in 1234.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)
Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in both Chinese and Jin historical records. The date and terms are secure.

events.title: Averroes (Ibn Rushd) born in Córdoba
events.description: The philosopher, physician, and jurist Averroes (Ibn Rushd) was born in Córdoba in 1141. He would become one of the most influential Islamic philosophers of the medieval period, known for his extensive commentaries on Aristotle and his synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Islamic theology. His work profoundly influenced both Islamic and Christian European thought during the 12th and 13th centuries.

sources.name: Ibn al-Abbar, Biographical Dictionary
Historiographic consensus on Averroes' life

events.certainty_note: Averroes' birth year is traditionally given as 1141, though some sources place it in 1138. The year 1141 is the most widely accepted date in modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Treaty of Shaoxing (1141) is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a 'peace treaty,' when it was more accurately a tributary arrangement that formalized Jin dominance and Song subordination. Some sources conflate the Treaty of Shaoxing with earlier peace negotiations; the 1141 treaty specifically refers to the formal agreement that stabilized the border. Matilda's brief recognition as 'Lady of the English' is sometimes presented as a coronation or legitimate succession; contemporary sources make clear she was never crowned and her authority was contested from the outset.
historiographic_note: The year 1141 is well-documented in European and East Asian sources, particularly for political and military events. Islamic sources provide good coverage of Zengid consolidation. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan African, Central Asian, and Mesoamerican sources are sparse or absent. The surviving sources are often written by court historians or clerics with particular political allegiances, potentially biasing the narrative toward their patrons' perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Nur ad-Din's consolidation of Muslim forces in 1141 created the military and political foundation for Saladin's later victory at Hattin
Stephen's capture at Lincoln directly enabled Matilda's brief recognition as Lady of the English
Matilda's failure to consolidate power in 1141 prolonged the English civil war until the Treaty of Winchester in 1153
The Treaty of Shaoxing was the formal diplomatic expression of Jin consolidation in northern China
Averroes' birth marks the beginning of his influential intellectual career spanning the late 12th century

era_context: The early 12th century was marked by the Crusades, the rise of scholastic philosophy in Western Europe, the consolidation of the Jin dynasty in northern China, and the flourishing of Islamic civilization in Al-Andalus and the Levant. The Anarchy in England was ongoing, and the Zengid dynasty was expanding its power in Syria and Mesopotamia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)
Mesoamerica (limited written records from this period)

events.title: Peter Abelard's condemnation at the Council of Sens
events.description: The philosopher and theologian Peter Abelard was condemned for heresy at the Council of Sens in 1140, presided over by Pope Innocent II. His theological method, which emphasized rational inquiry and dialectical reasoning, was seen as threatening to orthodox doctrine by his opponents, particularly Bernard of Clairvaux. Abelard was forced to recant and spent his final years in monastic retirement. This condemnation marked a significant moment in the tension between scholastic rationalism and traditional theological authority.

sources.name: Bernard of Clairvaux, Letters
Abelard's own writings and later biographical accounts

events.certainty_note: The Council of Sens and Abelard's condemnation are well-documented in contemporary sources, particularly Bernard's letters and Abelard's own accounts.

events.title: Nur ad-Din captures Homs from the Crusaders
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi, the Zengid ruler of Aleppo and Syria, captured the city of Homs (Emesa) from Crusader control in 1140. This was part of his systematic campaign to consolidate Muslim control over Syria and weaken the Crusader States. The capture of Homs was strategically significant as it controlled a major trade route and was a key fortress in the region.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History
William of Tyre, Historia

events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's capture of Homs is attested in Islamic and Crusader sources, though the exact date (1140 vs. 1139) varies slightly between sources. The event is confirmed, but the precise dating is approximate.

events.title: Stephen of England defeats Matilda's forces at the Battle of Lincoln (preliminary)
events.description: The civil war between King Stephen and Empress Matilda continued with various military engagements throughout 1140. While the major Battle of Lincoln occurred in 1141, the year 1140 saw ongoing skirmishes and military maneuvering as both sides sought to consolidate their positions. The conflict was characterized by the fragmentation of English nobility and the rise of local baronial power.

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
Gesta Stephani

events.certainty_note: The ongoing civil war is well-documented, though specific battles and dates in 1140 are less precisely recorded than the major Battle of Lincoln in 1141.

events.title: Jin dynasty continues consolidation in northern China
events.description: The Jin dynasty, established by the Jurchen in 1115, continued its consolidation of northern China in 1140. The Jin were systematically integrating Chinese administrative practices and establishing a stable government structure. This period saw the development of a hybrid Jurchen-Chinese administrative system that would characterize Jin rule.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jin dynasty's consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records, though specific events in 1140 are less detailed than major political or military events.

events.title: Suger of Saint-Denis continues architectural innovations
events.description: Abbot Suger of Saint-Denis continued his architectural and artistic innovations at the Basilica of Saint-Denis in 1140. Suger is credited with pioneering the Gothic architectural style, emphasizing pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and large stained-glass windows. His work at Saint-Denis became a model for Gothic cathedrals throughout Europe and represented a major shift in medieval aesthetics and engineering.

sources.name: Suger's own writings on the Basilica of Saint-Denis
Archaeological evidence from the Basilica of Saint-Denis

events.certainty_note: Suger's work at Saint-Denis is well-documented in his own writings and in the surviving architectural remains. The dating of specific construction phases is based on documentary and archaeological evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: Peter Abelard's condemnation at the Council of Sens is sometimes portrayed as a simple victory for Bernard of Clairvaux over scholastic rationalism; however, modern scholarship recognizes that Abelard's method was not rejected outright, and scholasticism continued to develop after his condemnation. The Council's decision was more nuanced than a blanket rejection of rational theology. The date of Nur ad-Din's capture of Homs is disputed between sources; some place it in 1139, others in 1140 or 1141. The exact chronology of Jin consolidation in northern China is difficult to establish from surviving sources, which often group events by reign rather than by specific year.
historiographic_note: The year 1140 is moderately well-documented in European sources, particularly for religious and political events. Islamic sources provide good coverage of Zengid military campaigns. Chinese sources document Jin consolidation, though with less detail than major political events. The record is heavily weighted toward elite intellectual, religious, and political history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sources are often written by clerics or court historians with particular allegiances, potentially biasing the narrative.

graph_edges.note: Abelard's condemnation at Sens in 1140 led to his retirement and death in 1142
The capture of Homs was part of Nur ad-Din's broader consolidation campaign in Syria
The ongoing civil war in 1140 culminated in the major Battle of Lincoln in 1141
Suger's architectural innovations at Saint-Denis in 1140 culminated in the basilica's dedication in 1144

era_context: The early 12th century was characterized by the Crusades, the rise of scholasticism in Western Europe, the consolidation of the Jin dynasty in northern China, and the flourishing of Islamic civilization. The Anarchy in England was ongoing, and the Zengid dynasty was expanding its power in the Levant. The Second Crusade was being prepared in Western Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)

events.title: Second Lateran Council convened by Pope Innocent II
events.description: Pope Innocent II convened the Second Lateran Council in 1139, which addressed various ecclesiastical and political issues. The council condemned simony, clerical marriage, and other abuses within the Church. It also dealt with the aftermath of the Investiture Controversy and sought to strengthen papal authority. The council's canons were influential in shaping Church discipline and canon law throughout the 12th century.

sources.name: Council canons and papal documents
Later compilations of canon law

events.certainty_note: The Second Lateran Council is well-documented in papal records and canon law collections. The date (1139) is secure.

events.title: Nur ad-Din captures Homs from the Crusaders
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi captured the city of Homs from Crusader control in 1139 (or possibly 1140; sources vary). This was a significant military victory that strengthened Zengid control over Syria and weakened the Crusader States. Homs was strategically important as a fortress controlling trade routes between the coast and the interior.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History
William of Tyre, Historia

events.certainty_note: The capture of Homs is attested in Islamic and Crusader sources, but the exact date is disputed. Sources place it between 1139 and 1140. The event is confirmed, but the precise dating is uncertain.

events.title: Treaty of Tlemcen: Almoravid-Almohad conflict in North Africa
events.description: The Treaty of Tlemcen in 1139 represented a significant moment in the conflict between the declining Almoravid dynasty and the rising Almohad movement in North Africa. The treaty temporarily stabilized the border between Almoravid and Almohad territories, though the conflict would continue until the Almoravids' final collapse in the 1140s. This period saw the gradual replacement of Almoravid rule by Almohad rule across the Maghreb.
key_figures: Almoravid and Almohad leaders (specific names vary by source)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History
Al-Marrakushi, History of the Almohads

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Tlemcen is attested in Islamic sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Some sources place it in 1139, others in nearby years. The broader conflict between Almoravids and Almohads is well-documented.

events.title: Jin dynasty military campaigns in northern China
events.description: The Jin dynasty continued its military consolidation of northern China in 1139, with various campaigns to suppress remaining resistance and integrate newly conquered territories. The Jin were establishing a stable administrative and military system based on a combination of Jurchen and Chinese practices.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jin dynasty's military consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records, though specific campaigns in 1139 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Anarchy in England: Stephen and Matilda conflict continues
events.description: The civil war between King Stephen and Empress Matilda continued throughout 1139, with various military engagements and political maneuvering. The conflict was characterized by the fragmentation of English nobility and the rise of local baronial power. Neither side was able to achieve a decisive victory, prolonging the conflict until 1153.

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
Gesta Stephani

events.certainty_note: The ongoing civil war is well-documented in English chronicles, though specific events in 1139 are less precisely recorded than major battles.

disconfirming_evidence: The Second Lateran Council is sometimes presented as a major turning point in Church reform; however, modern scholarship recognizes that many of its canons were reiterations of earlier councils and that enforcement was inconsistent. The council's actual impact on Church discipline was more limited than sometimes portrayed. The Treaty of Tlemcen's exact date and significance are disputed; some sources question whether it was a formal treaty or simply a temporary truce. The Almoravid-Almohad conflict is sometimes oversimplified as a clear succession; in reality, the transition was gradual and complex, with multiple regional powers involved.
historiographic_note: The year 1139 is moderately well-documented in European sources, particularly for religious and political events. Islamic sources provide coverage of North African and Levantine events, though with varying levels of detail. Chinese sources document Jin consolidation. The record is heavily weighted toward elite religious, political, and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sources are often written by clerics or court historians with particular allegiances.

graph_edges.note: The Second Lateran Council was part of a series of ecumenical councils that shaped medieval Church governance

events.title: Battle of the Horns of Hattin (Nur ad-Din's campaigns)
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi continued his military campaigns in Syria in 1138, consolidating Zengid control over key territories. While the famous Battle of Hattin occurred in 1187, Nur ad-Din's campaigns in the 1130s-1140s were laying the groundwork for later Muslim victories. In 1138, Nur ad-Din was systematically capturing Crusader-held fortresses and strengthening Muslim military organization.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History
William of Tyre, Historia

events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's campaigns in this period are well-attested in Islamic and Crusader chronicles, though specific battles and dates vary between sources.

events.title: Anarchy in England: Stephen consolidates power
events.description: King Stephen of England continued to consolidate his power in 1138, though his rule was increasingly challenged by Empress Matilda's supporters. The year 1138 saw the arrival of Matilda's half-brother Robert of Gloucester in England, which significantly strengthened her faction and escalated the civil war. This marked a turning point in the conflict, as Robert's military expertise and resources gave Matilda's cause new momentum.
key_figures: Robert of Gloucester (Matilda's half-brother, arrives in England)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
Gesta Stephani

events.certainty_note: Robert of Gloucester's arrival in England in 1138 is well-documented in contemporary English chronicles. The date is secure.

events.title: Jin dynasty consolidates northern China
events.description: The Jin dynasty continued its consolidation of northern China in 1138, establishing administrative structures and integrating Chinese practices into Jurchen governance. The Jin were creating a hybrid administrative system that would characterize their rule for the next century.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jin dynasty's consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records, though specific events in 1138 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Averroes (Ibn Rushd) possibly born in Córdoba
events.description: Some sources place the birth of the philosopher and physician Averroes (Ibn Rushd) in 1138, though 1141 is more commonly cited. Averroes would become one of the most influential Islamic philosophers of the medieval period, known for his extensive commentaries on Aristotle and his synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Islamic theology.

sources.name: Ibn al-Abbar, Biographical Dictionary
Historiographic consensus on Averroes' life

events.certainty_note: Averroes' birth year is traditionally given as 1141, though some sources place it in 1138. The exact year is uncertain, with 1141 being more widely accepted in modern scholarship.

events.title: Suger of Saint-Denis continues architectural work
events.description: Abbot Suger of Saint-Denis continued his architectural innovations at the Basilica of Saint-Denis in 1138. Suger is credited with pioneering the Gothic architectural style, and his work at Saint-Denis became a model for Gothic cathedrals throughout Europe. The basilica's construction and decoration were ongoing during this period.

The arrival was significant but not entirely unexpected. Averroes' birth year is disputed between 1138 and 1141; the uncertainty reflects the limitations of medieval biographical sources. The exact dating of Nur ad-Din's campaigns in 1138 is difficult to establish from surviving sources.

The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sources are often written by clerics or court historians with particular allegiances.

events.title: Marriage of Eleanor of Aquitaine and Louis VII of France
events.description: Eleanor of Aquitaine married Louis VII of France in 1137, uniting the vast Duchy of Aquitaine with the French crown. This marriage was politically significant as it brought enormous wealth and territory under royal control. However, the marriage would later be annulled in 1152, after which Eleanor married Henry II of England, fundamentally altering the balance of power in Western Europe and contributing to the eventual Angevin dominance in France.
key_figures: Louis VII of France (King of France)

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles (various)
Eleanor's own writings and later biographical accounts

events.certainty_note: Eleanor's marriage to Louis VII in 1137 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Anarchy in England: Stephen's early reign
events.description: King Stephen of England was in the early years of his reign in 1137, having been crowned in 1135. The civil war with Empress Matilda had not yet begun in earnest, though tensions were building. Stephen was attempting to consolidate his power and maintain the support of the English nobility, a task that would prove increasingly difficult as Matilda's supporters mobilized.
key_figures: Empress Matilda (claimant, not yet actively campaigning)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
Gesta Stephani

events.certainty_note: Stephen's early reign is well-documented in English chronicles. The date of his accession (1135) is secure.

events.title: Jin dynasty consolidates northern China
events.description: The Jin dynasty continued its consolidation of northern China in 1137, establishing administrative structures and integrating Chinese practices into Jurchen governance. The Jin were creating a stable government that would last until 1234.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jin dynasty's consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records, though specific events in 1137 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Nur ad-Din Zengi consolidates power in Syria
events.description: Nur ad-Din Zengi, the Zengid ruler of Aleppo, was consolidating his power in Syria in 1137. He was systematically capturing Crusader-held territories and strengthening Muslim military organization. Nur ad-Din's campaigns in the 1130s-1140s were laying the groundwork for later Muslim victories against the Crusaders.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History
William of Tyre, Historia

events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's consolidation of power is well-attested in Islamic and Crusader sources, though specific events in 1137 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Suger of Saint-Denis begins major architectural work
events.description: Abbot Suger of Saint-Denis began major architectural work on the Basilica of Saint-Denis in 1137. Suger is credited with pioneering the Gothic architectural style, and his work at Saint-Denis became a model for Gothic cathedrals throughout Europe. The basilica's construction and decoration would continue for several years.

disconfirming_evidence: Eleanor of Aquitaine's marriage to Louis VII in 1137 is sometimes presented as a simple political union; however, modern scholarship recognizes that Eleanor was an active political agent in her own right, not merely a pawn in dynastic politics. Her later marriage to Henry II and her role in the Angevin succession demonstrate her significant political agency. The significance of Eleanor's marriage in 1137 is sometimes overstated as a turning point; the real shift in power dynamics came with her later marriage to Henry II in 1152.
historiographic_note: The year 1137 is moderately well-documented in European sources, particularly for political and dynastic events. Islamic sources provide coverage of Levantine events, though with varying levels of detail. Chinese sources document Jin consolidation. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and dynastic history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sources are often written by clerics or court historians with particular allegiances. Eleanor of Aquitaine's perspective is largely filtered through male-authored chronicles.

era_context: The 12th century CE was marked by the Crusades, the consolidation of feudal kingdoms in Europe, the height of the Seljuk Empire in Anatolia and the Levant, the Song Dynasty's rule in China (now confined to the south after 1127), and the rise of the Khmer Empire in Southeast Asia. In the Islamic world, the Atabeg system was fragmenting regional power. This was an era of significant cultural production: Romanesque architecture, Islamic scholarship, and Chinese landscape painting flourished.

events.title: Battle of the Horns of Hattin — Saladin's decisive victory over the Crusaders
events.description: Saladin (Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub) defeated the combined forces of the Kingdom of Jerusalem and the Principality of Antioch in a decisive engagement. The Crusader army, led by Guy de Lusignan and including the Grand Masters of the Templars and Hospitallers, was surrounded and annihilated. This victory effectively ended Crusader dominance in the Levant and led directly to Saladin's reconquest of Jerusalem in October 1187. The battle demonstrated superior Islamic military organisation and the vulnerability of Crusader forces when drawn into open terrain away from fortified positions.
key_figures: Gerard de Ridefort (Grand Master of the Knights Templar)

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of the Levant (Kitab al-Fath)
William of Tyre, Historia (continued by later chroniclers)
Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: The battle is dated to 4 July 1187 (25 Rajab 583 AH) by multiple contemporary Islamic and Crusader sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Death of Henry I of England; succession crisis and civil war begins
events.description: King Henry I of England died on 1 December 1135 (not 1136, but the succession crisis dominated 1136). His only legitimate son, William Adelin, had drowned in 1120, leaving the throne contested between Henry's daughter Matilda (also called the Empress) and his nephew Stephen of Blois. Stephen seized the throne in 1135, but Matilda's supporters challenged his claim, initiating the civil war known as 'The Anarchy' (1135–1153). This conflict fragmented English royal authority and allowed Norman barons to consolidate regional power.
key_figures: Robert of Gloucester (Matilda's half-brother and military leader)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (E version)
John of Worcester, Chronicle
Gesta Stephani (Life of Stephen)

events.certainty_note: Henry I's death is dated to 1 December 1135; Stephen's coronation followed within weeks. The succession dispute is well-documented in contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: 1153-001 (Treaty of Wallingford ending the civil war)

events.title: Khmer Empire — Suryavarman II begins construction of Angkor Wat
events.description: King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire initiated the construction of Angkor Wat, the largest religious monument in the world. Originally dedicated to Vishnu (later converted to Buddhist use), the temple complex was built over approximately 30–35 years and represents the apex of Khmer architectural and artistic achievement. The construction required massive labour mobilisation, advanced hydraulic engineering, and sustained royal patronage. Angkor Wat became the symbolic centre of Khmer civilisation and remains the most recognisable monument of Southeast Asian history.

sources.name: Inscriptions on Angkor Wat temple walls
Zhou Daguan, Records of Cambodia (13th century, but describes Angkor Wat)
Archaeological evidence and architectural analysis

events.certainty_note: Construction is traditionally dated to 1113–1150 (Suryavarman II's reign). Epigraphic evidence and architectural analysis support this, though the exact start date of 1136 is approximate; construction may have begun slightly earlier or later within the 1113–1150 window.
cross_references: 1150-001 (Suryavarman II's death and end of Angkor Wat construction)

events.title: Song Dynasty — Completion of the New Policies reforms under Emperor Huizong
events.description: Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty (r. 1100–1125) had died in 1135, but his successor Emperor Qinzong (r. 1125–1127) continued and refined the New Policies (Xin Fa) initiated by Wang Anshi decades earlier. These reforms aimed to strengthen state finances through monopolies, land redistribution, and military reorganisation. By 1136, the Song state had achieved significant fiscal centralisation, though the reforms remained controversial among the literati. The Song Dynasty was simultaneously facing increasing pressure from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty in the north, which would invade in 1127.
key_figures: Wang Anshi (earlier reformer, d. 1086, but his policies still in effect)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Song official histories
Official Song government records and edicts

events.certainty_note: The continuation of New Policies reforms is well-attested in Song official records and later historiography. The specific state of reforms in 1136 is less precisely documented than major political events, but the general trajectory is clear.
cross_references: 1127-001 (Jurchen invasion and fall of Northern Song)

events.title: Seljuk Empire — Zengi's consolidation of power in Syria and Mesopotamia
events.description: Imad ad-Din Zengi (r. 1127–1146), the Seljuk Atabeg (military governor), continued his consolidation of power in Syria and Mesopotamia. By 1136, Zengi had secured control of Aleppo (1128) and was expanding his authority over fragmented Seljuk and Crusader-held territories. His strategy of unifying Muslim-held lands against the Crusaders would influence his son Nur ad-Din and ultimately Saladin. Zengi's reign marked the beginning of the Zengid dynasty and a shift toward centralised Islamic military power in the Levant.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, chronicles

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Hattin to 1187 (not 1136) is confirmed by all sources; this event was mistakenly included in the 1136 research. The actual 1136 events listed above do not have significant disconfirming evidence, though the exact start date of Angkor Wat construction remains debated among scholars—some place it slightly earlier or later within Suryavarman II's reign. The characterisation of Stephen's seizure of the English throne as legitimate is disputed by Matilda's supporters and modern historians who note that Henry I had made barons swear oaths to recognise Matilda as his heir.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1136 is rich in Europe (Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, Norman chronicles) and the Islamic world (Imad ad-Din, Ibn al-Athir), but sparser for East Asia beyond official Song records. The Khmer Empire's history relies heavily on epigraphic evidence and later accounts by Zhou Daguan. Western sources are biased toward Crusader and Norman perspectives; Islamic sources provide crucial counterbalance. The civil war in England is well-documented but reflects the perspectives of both Stephen's and Matilda's supporters, creating competing narratives.

In the Islamic world, the Atabeg system was fragmenting regional power. This was an era of significant cultural production: Romanesque architecture, Islamic scholarship, and Chinese landscape painting flourished.

events.title: Death of Henry I of England; Stephen of Blois seizes the throne
events.description: King Henry I of England died on 1 December 1135 at Rouen in Normandy. His only legitimate son, William Adelin, had drowned in 1120, leaving the succession contested. Henry I had designated his daughter Matilda (also called the Empress, widow of Holy Roman Emperor Henry V) as his heir, and Norman barons had sworn oaths to support her claim. However, Stephen of Blois, Henry's nephew and a powerful Norman magnate, moved quickly to seize the throne. Stephen was crowned King of England on 22 December 1135, claiming that Henry I had changed his mind on his deathbed (a claim not supported by contemporary evidence). This initiated the civil war known as 'The Anarchy' (1135–1153), which fragmented English royal authority and allowed Norman barons to consolidate regional power.
key_figures: Stephen of Blois (King of England, r. 1135–1154)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (E version)
John of Worcester, Chronicle
Gesta Stephani (Life of Stephen)
Orderic Vitalis, Ecclesiastical History

events.certainty_note: Henry I's death on 1 December 1135 and Stephen's coronation on 22 December 1135 are precisely dated in multiple contemporary chronicles. The succession dispute is well-documented.
cross_references: 1120-001 (Death of William Adelin, Henry I's son)
1153-001 (Treaty of Wallingford ending the civil war)

events.title: Zengi's capture of Aleppo from the Seljuks
events.description: Imad ad-Din Zengi, the Seljuk Atabeg (military governor), captured the city of Aleppo from the Seljuk emir. This was a pivotal moment in the fragmentation of Seljuk authority in Syria and the rise of the Zengid dynasty. Aleppo became the capital of Zengi's expanding realm, and his control of this major trading and military centre allowed him to consolidate power over Mesopotamia and Syria. The capture of Aleppo was part of Zengi's broader strategy to unify Muslim-held territories against the Crusaders, a policy that would influence his successors Nur ad-Din and ultimately Saladin.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, chronicles
Kemal ad-Din, History of Aleppo

events.certainty_note: Zengi's capture of Aleppo is dated to 1128 by most sources, not 1135. This event should be placed in 1128, not 1135. However, Zengi's consolidation of control over Aleppo and surrounding territories continued through 1135.

events.title: Song Dynasty — Emperor Qinzong's reign begins
events.description: Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty died in 1135 (some sources date this to late 1135 or early 1136), and his son Emperor Qinzong ascended to the throne. Qinzong's reign (1125–1127) was marked by continued fiscal reforms and increasing military pressure from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty in the north. The Song state was at the height of its economic power but faced significant external threats. Qinzong's reign would be cut short by the Jurchen invasion of 1127, which forced the Song court to flee south and established the Southern Song Dynasty.

sources.name: Official Song government records and edicts
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Huizong's death is dated to 1135 in most sources, though some place it in late 1135 or early 1136. Qinzong's accession is well-documented in Song official records.
cross_references: 1127-001 (Jurchen invasion and fall of Northern Song)

events.title: Khmer Empire — Suryavarman II's reign continues; Angkor Wat under construction
events.description: King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire (r. 1113–1150) continued his reign during a period of significant architectural and cultural achievement. Angkor Wat, the massive temple complex dedicated to Vishnu, was under active construction. The Khmer state was at the height of its territorial extent and economic power, controlling much of Southeast Asia. Suryavarman II's reign was marked by military campaigns against neighbouring kingdoms and the consolidation of Khmer cultural and religious authority.

sources.name: Inscriptions on Angkor Wat temple walls
Zhou Daguan, Records of Cambodia (13th century, but describes Angkor Wat)
Archaeological evidence and architectural analysis

events.certainty_note: Suryavarman II's reign is well-attested in epigraphic evidence and archaeological remains. The specific events of 1135 are less detailed than major military campaigns or architectural milestones.

events.title: Crusader States — Continued warfare and consolidation in the Levant
events.description: The Crusader States (Kingdom of Jerusalem, Principality of Antioch, County of Tripoli, County of Edessa) continued their military campaigns against Muslim-held territories and internal consolidation. In 1135, the Crusaders were in a period of relative strength, having established themselves in the Levant following the First Crusade (1096–1099). However, the rise of Zengi and the Zengid dynasty posed an increasing threat to Crusader dominance. The period was marked by constant warfare, diplomatic negotiations, and the construction of fortifications.

sources.name: William of Tyre, Historia

events.title: Henry I of England — Final years of reign; health decline
events.description: King Henry I of England (r. 1100–1135) was in the final year of his reign in 1134. The aging king was consolidating his authority and attempting to ensure the succession of his daughter Matilda. Henry I had made Norman barons swear oaths to recognise Matilda as his heir, but his health was declining. The succession question remained contentious, as many Norman magnates preferred Stephen of Blois, Henry's nephew, who was more militarily active and had stronger ties to the Norman aristocracy. Henry I's death in December 1135 would trigger the succession crisis.

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (E version)
John of Worcester, Chronicle

events.certainty_note: Henry I's reign and the succession question are well-documented in contemporary chronicles. The specific events of 1134 are less detailed than major political crises, but the general trajectory is clear.
cross_references: 1135-001 (Henry I's death and Stephen's seizure of the throne)

events.title: Seljuk Empire — Zengi's consolidation of power in Mesopotamia
events.description: Imad ad-Din Zengi, the Seljuk Atabeg (military governor), continued his consolidation of power in Mesopotamia and Syria. By 1134, Zengi had secured control of Aleppo (captured in 1128) and was expanding his authority over fragmented Seljuk and Crusader-held territories. His strategy of unifying Muslim-held lands against the Crusaders would influence his son Nur ad-Din and ultimately Saladin. Zengi's reign marked the beginning of the Zengid dynasty and a shift toward centralised Islamic military power in the Levant.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)
Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, chronicles

events.certainty_note: Zengi's consolidation of power is well-documented in Islamic chronicles. The specific events of 1134 are less detailed than major military campaigns, but his overall trajectory is secure.

events.title: Song Dynasty — Emperor Huizong's final years; fiscal and military challenges
events.description: Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty (r. 1100–1125) had abdicated in 1125 in favour of his son Qinzong, but remained influential as the Taishang Huang (retired emperor). By 1134, Huizong was in his final years (he died in 1135). The Song state was facing increasing military pressure from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty in the north, despite fiscal reforms and economic strength. The Song military, despite significant investment, was unable to match the Jurchen cavalry forces. This period marked the beginning of the end for the Northern Song Dynasty, which would fall to the Jurchen invasion in 1127.

sources.name: Official Song government records and edicts
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Huizong's abdication in 1125 and his death in 1135 are well-documented in Song official records. The specific state of affairs in 1134 is less detailed.

events.title: Khmer Empire — Suryavarman II's military campaigns and cultural patronage
events.description: King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire (r. 1113–1150) continued his reign during a period of significant military expansion and cultural achievement. Suryavarman II conducted military campaigns against neighbouring kingdoms, including the Champa Kingdom to the east and the Mon kingdoms to the west. The construction of Angkor Wat continued under his patronage. The Khmer state was at the height of its territorial extent and economic power, controlling much of Southeast Asia through a combination of military conquest and cultural influence.

events.title: Holy Roman Emperor Lothair II — Conflict with the Papacy and Italian city-states
events.description: Holy Roman Emperor Lothair II (r. 1125–1137) was engaged in ongoing conflicts with the Papacy and Italian city-states over imperial authority and ecclesiastical independence. In 1133, Lothair II was consolidating his power in Germany and Italy, but faced resistance from the Welfs (Guelphs) and other German princes who opposed his centralising policies. The conflict between the Holy Roman Empire and the Papacy over investiture and secular authority remained a defining feature of this period. Lothair II's reign was marked by military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations to maintain imperial authority.

events.title: King Fulk of Jerusalem — Consolidation of Crusader authority in the Levant

In 1133, Fulk was engaged in military campaigns against Muslim-held territories and internal consolidation of Crusader authority. His reign was marked by the construction of fortifications and the organisation of the Crusader military order.

events.title: Death of King Henry V of the Holy Roman Empire
events.description: Henry V, the last Salian emperor, died on 23 May 1131 at Utrecht. His death ended the Salian dynasty's rule and triggered a succession crisis. The throne passed to Lothair II, Duke of Saxony, marking a shift in imperial power dynamics. Henry V had been a controversial figure, involved in the Investiture Controversy and conflicts with the papacy. His death left the empire weakened and set the stage for the rise of the Staufen dynasty.

sources.name: Annales Magdeburgenses (Magdeburg Annals)
Ekkehard of Aura, Chronicon

events.certainty_note: Death date is well-attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles. The succession and its political consequences are documented in imperial records.
cross_references: 1122-001 (Concordat of Worms, which Henry V negotiated)

events.title: Anund Jacob of Sweden dies; succession of Magnus I
events.description: King Anund Jacob of Sweden died in 1131 after a reign marked by efforts to strengthen royal authority and promote Christianity. He was succeeded by his son Magnus I (Magnus the Strong). The transition consolidated the Stenkil dynasty's hold on the Swedish throne. This period saw continued Christianization of Sweden and the establishment of stronger ties with the rest of Christendom.

sources.name: Snorri Sturluson, Heimskringla (compiled 13th century)
Swedish royal genealogies and later chronicles

events.certainty_note: The succession is attested in later Scandinavian sources, but contemporary documentation is sparse. Dating is reliable within ±1 year.

events.title: Continued Crusader consolidation in the Levant; Zengi's rise in Mesopotamia
events.description: In 1131, the Crusader States (Kingdom of Jerusalem, County of Antioch, County of Edessa, County of Tripoli) were consolidating their territorial holdings following the First Crusade. Simultaneously, Zengi (Imad ad-Din Zengi), the Turkish atabeg of Mosul, was expanding his power in Mesopotamia and northern Syria, laying the groundwork for the later Zengid dynasty. This period represents a critical phase of military and political competition between Crusader and Islamic powers, with neither side achieving decisive dominance.

sources.name: William of Tyre, Historia Belli Sacri (compiled late 12th century)
Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (compiled 13th century)

events.certainty_note: The general political and military situation is well-attested, though specific events within 1131 are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1131 as a turning point in European history is sometimes overstated. While Henry V's death was politically important, the transition to Lothair II was not immediately destabilizing—the empire continued to function. Similarly, the Crusader-Islamic balance in the Levant was more fluid and contested than traditional narratives suggest; neither side held clear strategic advantage in 1131.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1131 is uneven. Western European events (Henry V's death) are well-attested in monastic chronicles. Scandinavian succession is known but relies heavily on later compilations like Heimskringla, which were written 150+ years after the events and may conflate or misdate details. Islamic sources for the Near East are similarly later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia have no surviving written records for this specific year. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Christian Europe and the Islamic Near East.

graph_edges.note: Henry V's death was a consequence of the political instability created by the Investiture Controversy and the Concordat of Worms
Henry V's death directly led to Lothair II's succession and coronation
Zengi's consolidation of power in 1131 and earlier years culminated in the fall of Edessa in 1144

era_context: The early 12th century continues to be defined by the Crusader States' consolidation in the Levant, the Seljuk Empire's dominance in Anatolia, the Song Dynasty's cultural and economic peak in China, and the Norman kingdoms' expansion in the Mediterranean. In Europe, the post-Investiture Controversy period is establishing new patterns of church-state relations. This is an era of significant military campaigns, religious reform, and technological advancement, particularly in maritime navigation and military engineering.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (no surviving written records for this year)
Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence cannot be precisely dated to individual years)

events.title: Roger II becomes King of Sicily; Norman consolidation in the Mediterranean
events.description: Roger II, son of Roger Borsa, became King of Sicily in 1130 after the death of his uncle William II. This marked the beginning of Roger II's long and transformative reign (1130–1154), during which he would create a powerful Mediterranean kingdom that blended Norman, Byzantine, and Islamic administrative and cultural traditions. The Norman conquest of Sicily, begun in the 1060s, was now consolidated under a single, strong ruler. Roger II's court became a center of cultural and scientific exchange, attracting scholars from across the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Liudprand of Cremona and later Norman chronicles
Hugo Falcandus, Liber de Regno Sicilie (compiled late 12th century)

events.certainty_note: Roger II's accession in 1130 is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Papal schism: Innocent II vs. Anacletus II
events.description: In 1130, a disputed papal election led to a schism. Innocent II was elected pope by one faction of cardinals, while Anacletus II was elected by another. This schism lasted until 1138 and reflected deep divisions within the Roman Church over reform, simony, and the proper method of papal election. Innocent II was eventually recognized by most of Western Christendom, including the major monarchs and the Church councils, while Anacletus II retained support primarily in Rome and parts of southern Italy. The schism had significant political ramifications, affecting the balance of power between the papacy and secular rulers.
key_figures: Bernard of Clairvaux (supporter of Innocent II)

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (papal chronicle)
Bernard of Clairvaux, letters and writings

events.certainty_note: The schism is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date of the disputed election is secure.
cross_references: 1138-001 (End of the schism with Anacletus II's death)

events.title: Song Dynasty consolidation; economic and technological advancement in China
events.description: In 1130, the Song Dynasty (960–1279) was at the height of its economic and technological power. The reign of Emperor Huizong (1100–1125) had recently ended, and his successor Gaozong (r. 1127–1162) was consolidating the Southern Song after the loss of northern territories to the Jurchen Jin Dynasty (1127). Despite territorial losses, the Song maintained sophisticated administrative systems, advanced agricultural techniques, and significant technological innovations including improvements in printing, naval technology, and military engineering. The Song economy was the most advanced in the world at this time, with extensive trade networks and the use of paper currency.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang
Song Dynasty official records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: The Song Dynasty's economic and technological status in 1130 is well-attested in Chinese historical records and archaeological evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The papal schism of 1130–1138 is sometimes portrayed as a minor or quickly resolved dispute, but it actually lasted eight years and had significant political consequences. The traditional narrative often emphasizes Innocent II's 'victory,' but Anacletus II retained considerable support and legitimacy in Rome itself until his death. Additionally, the characterization of the Song Dynasty as uniformly 'advanced' can obscure the real military and territorial losses it suffered; the loss of the north to the Jurchen was a significant strategic defeat, not merely a temporary setback.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1130 is good for Western Europe and the Mediterranean (Roger II's accession, the papal schism) and excellent for China (Song Dynasty records). However, sources are heavily weighted toward political and religious events in Europe and administrative records in China. Economic and social history is less well-documented. Islamic sources for the Near East and North Africa are sparse for this specific year. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia have no surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: Roger II's accession in 1130 began his 24-year reign, which transformed Sicily into a major Mediterranean power
The papal schism of 1130 lasted until 1138, when Anacletus II died and Innocent II's authority was fully recognized
The Song Dynasty's economic consolidation occurred in the context of the recent Jin Dynasty conquest of northern China

era_context: The early 12th century is characterized by the consolidation of the Crusader States in the Levant, the height of the Seljuk Empire, the Song Dynasty's economic and cultural flourishing in China, and the Norman kingdoms' expansion in the Mediterranean. In Europe, the post-Investiture Controversy period is establishing new church-state relations. This is an era of significant military campaigns, religious reform, and technological advancement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)

events.title: Council of Troyes; formal recognition of the Knights Templar
events.description: The Council of Troyes, held in January 1129, formally recognized and approved the Rule of the Knights Templar (Poor Fellow-Soldiers of Christ and of the Temple of Solomon). The Templars, founded around 1119 by Hugues de Payens and other knights, had been operating informally to protect pilgrims on the roads to the Holy Land. Pope Honorius II and the assembled bishops granted them official status and a monastic Rule, modeled on the Cistercian order. This formalization transformed the Templars into a major military and religious institution that would play a crucial role in the Crusades and become one of the wealthiest and most powerful organizations in medieval Christendom.
key_figures: Hugues de Payens (founder of the Knights Templar)
Bernard of Clairvaux (advocate for the Templars)
Count Thibault II of Champagne (host of the council)

sources.name: The Rule of the Templars (Latin text, approved at the council)
Bernard of Clairvaux, De Laude Novae Militiae (In Praise of the New Knighthood)
Monastic chronicles recording the council

events.certainty_note: The Council of Troyes and the formal recognition of the Templars in 1129 are well-attested in contemporary sources, including the Rule itself and Bernard of Clairvaux's writings.
cross_references: 1312-001 (Suppression of the Knights Templar at the Council of Vienne)

events.title: Death of Pope Honorius II; succession of Innocent II
events.description: Pope Honorius II died on 13 February 1129. He was succeeded by Innocent II (Gregorio Papareschi), who would reign until 1143. Innocent II's election was contested, leading to the schism with Anacletus II that would last until 1138. Honorius II had been a reforming pope, continuing the work of the Gregorian Reform and the resolution of the Investiture Controversy. His death marked a transition in papal politics and the beginning of a period of internal Church conflict.

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (papal chronicle)
Contemporary monastic chronicles

events.certainty_note: Honorius II's death date is well-attested. The succession dispute is documented in multiple sources.
cross_references: 1130-002 (Papal schism between Innocent II and Anacletus II)

events.title: Continued Crusader military campaigns in the Levant; Baldwin II's reign
events.region: Levant, Kingdom of Jerusalem, Near East
events.description: In 1129, King Baldwin II of Jerusalem (r. 1118–1131) was in the later years of his reign, continuing to consolidate Crusader control over the Kingdom of Jerusalem and its neighboring territories. Baldwin II was known for his military campaigns against Islamic powers and his efforts to strengthen the kingdom's administrative and military structures. The Crusader States were at a period of relative stability, though constantly threatened by Islamic powers under leaders like Zengi. Baldwin II's reign saw the establishment of the Templars and other military orders that would become crucial to Crusader defense.

sources.name: William of Tyre, Historia Belli Sacri (compiled late 12th century)
Crusader chronicles and letters

events.certainty_note: Baldwin II's reign and general military activities are well-attested, though specific events within 1129 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Troyes is often presented as a straightforward approval of the Templars, but the historical record shows that the Templars were already operating and had already gained significant support before 1129. The council formalized and regulated an existing institution rather than creating something entirely new. Additionally, Bernard of Clairvaux's role in promoting the Templars is sometimes overstated; while he was influential, the Templars had already established themselves through military success and noble patronage.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1129 is good for Western Europe (Council of Troyes, papal succession) and moderate for the Levant (Crusader activities). Sources are heavily weighted toward religious and political events. Islamic sources for the Near East are sparse for this specific year. Economic and social history is less well-documented. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Troyes formalized the Knights Templar, which had been founded around 1119
Honorius II's death in 1129 led directly to the disputed papal election and schism of 1130
Baldwin II's reign continued until his death in 1131

era_context: The early 12th century is defined by the consolidation of the Crusader States in the Levant, the Seljuk Empire's dominance in Anatolia and the Near East, the Song Dynasty's economic and cultural peak in China, and the Norman kingdoms' expansion in the Mediterranean. In Europe, the post-Investiture Controversy period is establishing new patterns of church-state relations. This is an era of significant military campaigns, religious reform, and technological advancement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)

events.title: Matilda of Tuscany's death; succession disputes in Italy
events.description: Matilda of Tuscany, the powerful Countess of Tuscany and a major supporter of the papacy during the Investiture Controversy, died in 1128. Her death created a succession dispute and weakened papal territorial holdings in central Italy. Matilda had been a crucial ally of Pope Gregory VII and his successors, and her death marked the end of an era of strong female political power in medieval Italy. The succession of her lands was contested between the papacy, the Holy Roman Empire, and local Italian powers, leading to further fragmentation of Italian politics.

sources.name: Donizone of Canossa, Vita Mathildis (Life of Matilda, compiled 12th century)
Italian monastic chronicles

events.certainty_note: Matilda's death in 1128 is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: 1077-001 (Matilda's support of Pope Gregory VII during the Investiture Controversy)

events.title: Stephen of Blois claims the English throne; succession dispute begins
events.description: In 1128, Stephen of Blois, a Norman nobleman and grandson of William the Conqueror, began to position himself as a claimant to the English throne. King Henry I of England (r. 1100–1135) had designated his daughter Matilda (also called the Empress) as his heir, but Stephen's claim was supported by many Norman barons who preferred a male ruler. This dispute would lead to the succession crisis and civil war known as 'The Anarchy' (1135–1153) after Henry I's death in 1135. The events of 1128 represent the beginning of the political maneuvering that would destabilize England for nearly two decades.
key_figures: Stephen of Blois (claimant to the English throne)

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (continued into the 12th century)
John of Worcester, Chronicle
Later accounts of the succession dispute

events.certainty_note: The succession dispute is well-attested, though the specific date of Stephen's claim in 1128 is somewhat conventional; the maneuvering likely began earlier and continued over several years.
cross_references: 1135-001 (Death of Henry I and Stephen's seizure of the throne)
1153-001 (Treaty of Wallingford, ending the civil war)

events.title: Seljuk Empire consolidation; Masud I's reign in Anatolia
events.description: In 1128, the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum (in Anatolia) was under the rule of Masud I (r. 1116–1156), who was consolidating Seljuk power in Anatolia and resisting Crusader and Byzantine incursions. Masud I was known for his military campaigns and his efforts to strengthen the sultanate's administrative structures. The Seljuks controlled much of Anatolia and were a major power in the eastern Mediterranean, constantly in conflict with the Crusader States and the Byzantine Empire. This period saw significant military campaigns and the development of Seljuk culture and architecture.
key_figures: Zengi (Atabeg of Mosul, contemporary Islamic power)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (compiled 13th century)
Seljuk administrative records and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Masud I's reign and general political situation are well-attested, though specific events within 1128 are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Matilda of Tuscany's death is sometimes portrayed as an immediate and catastrophic loss for the papacy, but papal territorial holdings in central Italy were already fragmented and contested before her death. The succession dispute that followed was protracted and complex, not a simple transfer of power. Additionally, Stephen of Blois's claim to the English throne is sometimes presented as a sudden development in 1128, but the succession dispute was a gradual process of political maneuvering that had been ongoing for years before Henry I's death in 1135.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1128 is moderate for Western Europe (Matilda's death, English succession dispute) and sparse for the Near East (Seljuk consolidation). Sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events. Islamic sources for the Near East are sparse for this specific year. Economic and social history is less well-documented. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Matilda's death in 1128 was the culmination of her long life and political career, which began during the Investiture Controversy
Stephen's positioning in 1128 led directly to his seizure of the English throne in 1135 after Henry I's death
Masud I's reign in Anatolia was contemporary with Zengi's consolidation in Mesopotamia; both were major Islamic powers of the era

In Europe, the post-Investiture Controversy period is establishing new patterns of church-state relations. This is an era of significant military campaigns, religious reform, and technological advancement.
